title: Lynnwood
name: Lynnwood
metaDescription: 1031 exchange coordination for Lynnwood's Alderwood Mall retail, Link light rail terminus multifamily, and I-5 corridor near Seattle.

sections.html: Lynnwood anchors the northern end of the metro around Alderwood Mall and the newly opened Lynnwood City Center light rail station, and a buyer building a Lynnwood identification package should expect retail and transit-oriented multifamily to make up most of the submittal. A qualified intermediary coordinating the closing should confirm a target property's actual walking distance to the station before finalizing an identification, since the light rail extension's opening has already begun to separate station-adjacent product from the rest of Lynnwood's inventory, and that separation is likely to widen further as more station-area development delivers.

sections.heading: Alderwood Mall and the Surrounding Retail Base
sections.html: Alderwood Mall anchors Lynnwood's retail base as a regional draw pulling shoppers from Everett, Edmonds, and the surrounding suburbs, and the retail pads and smaller centers around it lease at rents that track the mall's traffic more closely than a standalone neighborhood center would. A buyer identifying retail near Alderwood should confirm whether a target property benefits directly from mall traffic or sits far enough away to trade on its own local demand instead. Anchor and junior-anchor tenant health at the mall itself also affects the smaller surrounding pads, so a buyer should check current occupancy at the mall before assuming a nearby pad's traffic will hold steady, since a vacant anchor space can measurably soften foot traffic at outparcels that otherwise look stable on paper.

sections.heading: Transit-Oriented Multifamily at the Link Terminus
sections.html:
The Link light rail extension to Lynnwood City Center opened recently, and multifamily development immediately surrounding the station has accelerated as developers target renters commuting into Seattle without a car. A buyer replacing into this submarket should confirm a target building's actual distance to the station platform, since the transit-oriented premium concentrates within a short walking radius and drops off meaningfully beyond it.

The statewide rent-cap law applies to this new station-area product the same as older Lynnwood stock, and a buyer should model rent growth against the cap rather than assuming new transit access alone justifies aggressive rent projections.

sections.heading: I-5 Corridor Office and Light-Industrial Space
sections.html: Lynnwood's stretch along I-5 also carries a smaller base of suburban office and light-industrial buildings serving regional service and distribution tenants, and this product trades independently of both the mall retail and the new transit-oriented multifamily, on more conventional suburban fundamentals. A buyer should keep this corridor's pricing separate from the station-area premium rather than blending the two into a single Lynnwood average. This I-5 stock tends to trade at a lower basis than either the mall retail or the station-area multifamily, which can make it a workable option for a buyer managing boot exposure on a downsizing exchange.

sections.heading: Building the Lynnwood Identification List
sections.html:
A realistic Lynnwood submittal typically draws from:

  • retail pads and centers near Alderwood Mall
  • transit-oriented multifamily near the Lynnwood City Center station
  • suburban office and light-industrial space along I-5
  • neighborhood retail serving the surrounding residential population

sections.heading: Confirming Station Distance Before Finalizing an Identification
sections.html:
Because the transit-oriented premium in Lynnwood is so tightly tied to actual walking distance from the station, a buyer should verify that distance directly rather than relying on a listing description of a property as station-adjacent, and should raise any discrepancy with a qualified intermediary before the 45-day identification window closes, since exchange proceeds must also stay with the intermediary throughout the closing to avoid constructive receipt by the taxpayer.

Buyers often pair a Lynnwood station-area identification with an Everett or Edmonds alternative on the same three-property list, so a valuation dispute over station distance on one candidate does not leave the exchange without a workable option inside the 180-day exchange period.

faqs.q: How does Alderwood Mall affect nearby retail pricing in Lynnwood?
faqs.a: Retail pads and smaller centers that benefit directly from mall traffic price differently than properties far enough away to trade on local demand alone, so a buyer should confirm which category a target property falls into, and should also check current anchor occupancy at the mall itself.

faqs.q: How far does the transit-oriented premium extend from the Lynnwood City Center station?
faqs.a: It concentrates within a short walking radius of the platform and drops off meaningfully beyond that, so a buyer should confirm a target building's actual distance rather than relying on a general station-adjacent description, and should expect that gap to widen further as more station-area development delivers over the coming years.

faqs.q: Does the statewide rent-cap law apply to new station-area multifamily in Lynnwood?
faqs.a: Yes, it applies to new and older multifamily stock alike, so a buyer should model rent growth against the cap rather than assuming new transit access alone justifies aggressive projections, since the cap sets the ceiling regardless of a building's proximity to the station.

faqs.q: Is Lynnwood's I-5 office and light-industrial space priced like the station area?
faqs.a: No, this corridor trades independently on conventional suburban fundamentals and should be priced separately from the transit-oriented premium near the station, and it often trades at a lower basis that can suit a buyer managing boot exposure on a downsizing exchange.

faqs.q: Why pair a Lynnwood identification with Everett or Edmonds?
faqs.a: If a valuation dispute over actual station distance arises on the Lynnwood candidate, having a nearby alternative on the same three-property list keeps the exchange workable inside the 180-day period, and both nearby cities offer their own retail or multifamily comparables that do not depend on the same station-distance premium.
